name: Embalse de Guadalcacín – circular from Arcos de la Frontera
name_original: Embalse de Guadalcacín – circular desde Arcos de la Frontera
region_intro: Road cycling around Arcos De La Frontera offers diverse terrain, from gentle rolling hills to challenging climbs, set amidst the picturesque white villages of Cádiz province. The region features a landscape of olive groves, vineyards, and dramatic river valleys, with routes often following quiet country roads. Cyclists can experience varied topography, including ascents into mountainous areas and paths alongside the Guadalete River. The Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park provides a rugged backdrop for many routes, characterized by its natural features. Best road cycling routes around Arcos De La Frontera The most popular road cycling route is Embalse de Guadalcacín – circular from Arcos de la Frontera, a 36.0 miles (58.0 km) trail that takes 2 hours 51 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers a scenic loop around the Guadalcacín reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Embalse de Bornos – circular from Arcos de la Frontera, a moderate 32.8 miles (52.8 km) path. This circular route provides views of the Bornos reservoir and surrounding countryside. Local road cyclists also love the Castillo de Melgarejo loop from Arcos de la Frontera, a 45.1 miles (72.6 km) trail leading through rolling countryside and past historical sites, often completed in about 3 hours 9 minutes. Road cycling around Arcos De La Frontera is defined by white villages, rolling hills, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging climbs. The routes in Arcos De La Frontera are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 900 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Arcos De La Frontera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Arcos de la Frontera?
A: The region offers a diverse cycling experience, from gentle rolling hills adorned with sunflower fields and vineyards to challenging climbs and descents, especially in routes traversing mountainous areas. You'll find quiet country roads, and some routes even follow the reconditioned disused railway, the Vía Verde, for a unique ride.

Q: Are there challenging climbs for experienced road cyclists in the area?
A: Yes, Arcos de la Frontera provides significant challenges for experienced cyclists. Passes like &lt;strong&gt;Puerto del Boyar&lt;/strong&gt; are known for their inclusion in the Vuelta a España. The region's topography includes abundant challenging climbs and descents, particularly in routes that venture into the mountainous parts of the Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or natural features I can see on road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer breathtaking panoramic views, such as those from the &lt;strong&gt;Las Palomas Pass&lt;/strong&gt;. You'll often ride high above the Guadalete River, providing spectacular vistas. The area is also characterized by the stunning landscapes of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4220058" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, with its rugged terrain and diverse forests.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes?
A: Yes, while there are challenging options, the region also caters to less experienced cyclists. There are 3 easy routes available, and many moderate options that offer pleasant rides through rolling countryside without extreme elevation. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974917536/embalse-de-guadalcacin-circular-from-arcos-de-la-frontera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Guadalcacín – circular from Arcos de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route offering a scenic loop.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Arcos de la Frontera?
A: The months of April and October are particularly popular for cycling in Arcos de la Frontera, offering pleasant temperatures and favorable conditions. The region generally enjoys good weather for outdoor activities for much of the year.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes around Arcos de la Frontera?
A: Absolutely. Many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Popular circular options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e971857696/embalse-de-bornos-circular-from-arcos-de-la-frontera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Bornos – circular from Arcos de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt;, which provides views of the reservoir and surrounding countryside.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can I explore along the road cycling routes?
A: Arcos de la Frontera is a prime example of the famous White Villages, and many routes pass through or near these distinctive towns. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1035009" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Town of Arcos de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt; with its historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4927992" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Basilica of Santa María de la Asunción&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6877288" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Melgarejo loop from Arcos de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt; is another route that leads past historical sites.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by waterfalls or significant natural monuments?
A: Yes, some routes may bring you close to natural features. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1050506" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Honda Waterfall of Benamahoma&lt;/a&gt; is a notable natural monument in the wider region that can be incorporated into longer rides or visited separately. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4220058" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Grazalema Natural Park&lt;/a&gt; itself is a significant natural draw.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Arcos de la Frontera?
A: The routes in Arcos de la Frontera are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.9 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the picturesque white villages, and the stunning views of the river valleys and reservoirs.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Arcos de la Frontera, and what are their difficulty levels?
A: There are 48 road cycling routes available around Arcos de la Frontera. These include 3 easy routes, 26 moderate routes, and 19 difficult routes, offering options for all skill levels.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the reservoirs near Arcos de la Frontera?
A: Yes, the region features beautiful reservoirs that are popular cycling destinations. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974917536/embalse-de-guadalcacin-circular-from-arcos-de-la-frontera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Guadalcacín – circular from Arcos de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e971857696/embalse-de-bornos-circular-from-arcos-de-la-frontera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Bornos – circular from Arcos de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt; are excellent examples, offering scenic rides around these water bodies.

Q: Can I find routes that are suitable for a longer, more challenging day out?
A: For those seeking a longer and more challenging ride, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1275508" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del Mojón de la Víbora – Picao Bridge loop from Las Abiertas&lt;/a&gt; cover nearly 94 km with significant elevation gain, providing a demanding and rewarding experience.
tips: At the end of a cul-de-sac there is a wonderful view of the hinterland and the Guadalete.

The Puerta Matrera is the only remaining gate of the original city wall of Arcos de la Frontera. This gate connects the old town centre with the Barrio Bajo. It is a historically important building that was restored in the 17th and 18th centuries. From here you can still see remains of the city wall between the houses. 
The gate also has a small chapel with a statue of the Virgen del Pilar, which has been recently restored.

This reservoir is the largest in the province of Cádiz. Called Guadalcacín II because it is built on the basis of the previous reservoir that had a capacity of only 76hm3; the current one is 800 hm3, it was designed after the 1993 drought.
Bridge in the Guadalcacín Reservoir, from here we can enjoy views of the swamp on one side and the Alcornocales Natural Park on the other
